macro_events.event_summary: Iran has reportedly closed the Strait of Hormuz again, firing on a vessel attempting transit — threatening ~20% of global seaborne oil and most Gulf LNG flows.
primary_drivers: Crude oil price spike from supply disruption risk (~17-20M bpd transits Hormuz)
Tanker rates surge on rerouting and war-risk premiums
Defense/geopolitical risk premium; flight to safety

beneficiaries.rationale: Integrated major with large non-Gulf production (Permian, Guyana, offshore); direct beneficiary of Brent spike with minimal Hormuz transit exposure on its own barrels.
Similar to XOM — Permian/US-heavy upstream realizes higher crude prices; Gulf exposure is smaller share of production mix.
Pure-play Permian producer with near-100% US onshore production — direct leverage to WTI spike with zero Hormuz transit risk.

Frontline — largest listed crude tanker operator (VLCC/Suezmax); Hormuz closure forces rerouting around Africa, war-risk insurance premiums spike, day rates jump sharply (historical analog: 2019 tanker attacks saw VLCC rates triple).
International Seaways — US-listed crude/product tanker operator directly leveraged to rerouting ton-mile demand and spot rate spikes.
Prime contractor for PAC-3/THAAD missile defense and F-35; Gulf escalation drives FMS demand from Saudi/UAE/Israel and supplemental US defense appropriations.
Raytheon makes Patriot, SM-3, Tomahawk — direct munitions resupply beneficiary if kinetic US response in Gulf; also GTF engine exposure to F-35 supply chain.

losers.rationale: Delta — jet fuel is ~25-30% of opex with limited hedging post-refinery; Brent spike compresses margin directly, plus any Gulf/Asia routing disruption.
United has largest international/Pacific exposure of US carriers — hit by both fuel cost surge and potential Gulf overflight disruptions on Asia routes.
American Airlines — unhedged jet fuel exposure, highly leveraged balance sheet amplifies margin hit from oil spike.
Carnival — bunker fuel is major opex line; also consumer discretionary travel demand softens on geopolitical fear and higher gas prices at pump.
Dow Chemical — naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with crude; commodity chemicals have limited pricing power to pass through quickly.
LyondellBasell — petrochemical producer with direct feedstock cost exposure to crude-linked naphtha; margin squeeze from input spike.
Walmart — diesel/transport costs feed into supply chain; low-income consumer (core customer) sees discretionary wallet squeezed by gasoline pump prices.
FedEx — jet fuel + diesel dual exposure; surcharge lag means near-term margin compression before pass-through.

headlines: Iran says Strait of Hormuz is closed again as vessel attempting to cross comes under gunfire

macro_events.event_summary: Post-Iran war commentary framing the US as the world's most secure energy power, implying sustained demand for US oil & gas exports and reduced geopolitical risk premium shifting toward US producers.
primary_drivers: Structural shift of global energy buyers toward US supply (Middle East supply risk premium now attached to the region, not the US)
Increased demand for US LNG export capacity from allies seeking secure supply
Likely supportive US policy backdrop for domestic production, pipelines, and export terminals

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere Energy is the largest US LNG exporter (Sabine Pass, Corpus Christi); a narrative of US as secure energy supplier directly drives long-term LNG offtake contracts from European and Asian utilities looking to diversify away from Middle East exposure.
Venture Global LNG — pure-play US LNG export developer (Calcasieu Pass, Plaquemines) with new capacity coming online; direct beneficiary of incremental allied demand for US-sourced gas.
Diamondback Energy is a pure-play Permian producer; a thesis that elevates US as the stable marginal barrel supplier supports volume growth and export-linked pricing for Permian crude.
EOG Resources — low-cost US shale producer with strong FCF; benefits from a structurally higher US share of global supply and any policy tailwind for domestic drilling.
Williams Companies operates Transco, the largest US natgas pipeline system feeding Gulf Coast LNG export terminals; rising export throughput translates directly into contracted volume growth.
Kinder Morgan — largest US natgas pipeline network; direct volume beneficiary of higher LNG feedgas demand tied to the 'secure US energy' thesis.
Texas Pacific Land Corp owns Permian surface/royalty rights; benefits from sustained US production growth without capex exposure — leveraged to volume, not price.

losers.rationale: Exxon has significant international/Middle East upstream exposure and benefits from high crude prices more than from US volume growth; a lower global risk premium compresses realized pricing even as US volumes rise — net neutral to slightly negative vs pure-play US shale.

Halliburton has outsized Middle East service revenue (~20%+); post-war reconstruction is uncertain and regional capex may pause, while a US-centric narrative favors peers more levered to North American land (e.g. LBRT).

headlines: The real outcome of the Iran war: America is now the world’s most secure energy power

macro_events.event_summary: Analyst argues that AI compute rationing (GPU scarcity/cost) is constraining AI-native competitors and benefiting traditional SaaS incumbents whose workloads don't require massive GPU capacity.
primary_drivers: GPU supply scarcity raising unit economics cost for AI-native challengers
Traditional SaaS margin defense as AI disruption threat is deferred
Capex discipline pressure on hyperscalers/AI labs

beneficiaries.rationale: Salesforce is the canonical 'traditional SaaS' incumbent facing AI-native CRM/agent disruption threats; compute rationing delays when startups like Sierra/Decagon can scale to threaten seat-based revenue. Narrative-driven multiple re-rating candidate.

Adobe has been the poster child of 'AI disruption fears' (vs Midjourney, Runway, OpenAI image/video). If generative AI competitors can't afford inference at scale, Adobe's Creative Cloud moat holds longer. Direct beneficiary of the exact thesis in the article.
ServiceNow is a traditional enterprise SaaS workflow platform; same incumbency argument — AI agent startups targeting ITSM workflows face compute cost walls.
Workday HR/Finance SaaS faces similar AI-agent displacement narratives; compute rationing extends runway for incumbent defense. Sentiment/rotation trade.
Intuit (TurboTax, QuickBooks) has been flagged as AI-disruption risk by standalone AI tax/accounting agents; those challengers are most compute-constrained.

losers.rationale: If the narrative shifts to 'AI companies are rationing compute because they can't afford it,' that questions sustainability of GPU demand at current pricing — direct pressure on Nvidia's forward revenue thesis. Headline-risk trade, not fundamental yet.
Palantir trades as an AI-native winner at premium multiple; any narrative that 'AI companies are compute-constrained vs traditional software' pressures the AI-premium stocks on relative rotation.
C3.ai is a pure-play AI-native software name; 'traditional SaaS wins' framing is a direct negative rotation signal.

headlines: AI companies are rationing compute. That’s a boon for traditional software, one analyst says.

macro_events.event_summary: A Yahoo Finance opinion/analysis piece citing experts warning that US recession risks are rising, with guidance for investor positioning.
primary_drivers: Commentary-level recession narrative with no new data — limited direct market impact
Potential mild defensive rotation if piece gets traction on social/financial media
No policy change, no earnings revision catalyst
headlines: Experts Warn That Recession Risks Are Increasing. Here's What That Means for Investors
